speaker: interviewer
text: It's Thursday August 10, 1995. My name is Gloria Perlman Schneider, and we are interviewing Matthew Guttman in Queens, New York. So Mr. Guttman, after you didn't find your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to Wels, Austria, to my former camp. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everything was going normally. I met my wife-- she was a young girl. I met her. She went to a dance because the Americans, they made music for the former inmates that they should recuperate mentally from all the bad dreams, the horrible dreams. So they made music, they made dances, they builded stages under the free sky in summertime. You know, beautiful. And she was a young child. She didn't dance so well, she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: One evening-- I became a policeman there, working with Americans together-- one-- she was going to the dance all by herself, and I was going to-- to go to service. And I'm passing by the dance place, and I says, where are you dancing? Are you going to dance with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, oh, I am six years older than she is, so she says to me, I am not dancing well. She says, that's why I'm dancing under the stage with the young children, because I'm not a good dancer. I says, nothing doing. So I said, OK. I went. We started dancing under the stage, and then I took on the stage, and we danced the next day. We met again, but she had a lot of boys where they were interested in her, younger-- younger boys than I was. And one day she's coming, because I was a policeman, and I was in the gate. You know, the-- the camp was closed on account of the sickness. Nobody could go, nobody could come in because the sickness was still going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says to me, she says, how could I go to the beach? I have a boy, what I'm a-- we are friends. He wants we should go to the beach, and I would like to go swimming a little bit. I says, you know what? I'm going to let you out. I am today in service. I am going to let you out, but I says, four hours later you have to come back because then you cannot come in. OK, an hour later she's back. I am seeing a my girl. I says, Margaret, I says, what are you doing? I says, an hour later you are here. Ah, they just want, all the boys just want one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she left him there, and she ran away. So she came in the camp, and she was still in the hospital. She was still in the hospital, but she was walking around. She was recuperated already, she was walking. But they kept her, as a child, they kept her in the hospital. And at night, we are sitting on a bench near the hospital, and she's coming out, too. And slowly by slowly we talk, we talk, and we talk. I tell her my story. And I see that she's crying, you know, that I had, you know, such a poor childhood. And she was crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked her she should marry me. She wasn't sure but she says, I had a sister, and if she's coming home, she's three years older than I am. If she's coming home, she's going to marry you. I says, who the hell are you? I says, maybe she doesn't want me, or maybe the other way around.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says then, I am going to marry you, because you're a good boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the way she became-- we became engaged. The UNRRA, the United Nations refugee organization, they brought by truckload the roses into the camp. We were the first engaged people after the war from this [INAUDIBLE]. We were the first engaged couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made us-- they gave us a party, a king size, and-- like-- like in a palace, with the roses everywhere. They went to the Germans, to the Austrians. You know, they brought the roses, all the roses in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, somebody came from my wife's country, from my wife's city, and tell her that her father and her sister supposed to be alive, and they're coming home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't come to America, because I was supposed to come to America. Because I was in the service, working with Americans, I year later would have gotten myself entry into United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this way, no more. We went home because, after all, her father and her sister were supposed to come home. We arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was your wife from? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carei. We arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania. We arrived on the border city, and we met a cousin of hers. Is it true that's my father and my sister is coming home? She says, not true. They're all dead. If we could, we would have run right away back into the American zone, Austria. Because whatever we saw there by the Russians, it was a horrible thing. Horrible. They didn't help themselves, nothing. We were really starving for hunger after the war in the old country. But I said, as soon the winter passes by, out we go. We crossed illegally the borders, the Romanian-Hungarian border. We came to Budapest. From there, we crossed the Austrian-Hungarian border. If the Russians would have caught us, we  would have wound up in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this way we came to the American zone in Austria. And our son, my first son, God rest him in peace, was born in Salzburg, Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came. We immigrated to Canada in 1947. They made us feel like home. Then another child was born, my son, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? What was your profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furrier. I worked as a furrier in Canada. I made a good living. I was happy-- well, of course, in the beginning it was hard. In the beginning, no language, no nothing at all. I'll never forget, on the trolley-- trolley bus sits an old man, talks to me in English. And I just make my head yes, and questions, yes or no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he sees that I answered in the wrong way, the yes and the no. [LAUGHS] He says to me, you don't speak English. I said, no. How long are you here? I said, two weeks. I knew that much. Then he says, you have to adjust yourself to the circumstances which you are living. I says to him, pardon, pardon, pardon? He imitated again. I wanted to chew it up. I should know to my landlady to ask her what that means?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, oh yes, he is right. Anyway, we immigrated to United States, and my catastrophe happened in Atlantic City, three days after my arrival. And here, a doctor suggested, he gave me the prescription we should have another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell about what happened in Atlantic City a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Atlantic City, we arrived. We bought before, in Atlantic City, we bought a building on Pacific Avenue. It was a diner, not a restaurant. And it was about 66 rooms and two apartments in the building. It was an older building. And in downstairs, all over the building was one diner, a big one. I came from Canada three days later. I started to open up, started working with people, through the same people where there were before. But I didn't sleep in the place on account because it wasn't heated yet. Monday they supposed to heat-- we arrived Thursday, and Monday morning they're supposed to come to install the heating system, after the winter. And we were sleeping by a friend of mine in the beach block. And the last Sunday night, around 1 o'clock, we woke up. We heard hollerings from downstairs-- fire! Fire! Fire! And then we hear an explosion. It was a gas explosion in the-- in the lobby. It was maybe 10 bedrooms, 10 bathrooms. Each bedroom had a bathroom, some, like a castle. My son was sleeping in one bedroom. My little girl was sleeping in another bedroom with this son of mine. But this here, he was always a nervous boy. He woke up, and when I hollered fire, he was right near me. It was dark-- it was no more light. It was full, the house, with smoke. And everything-- you were choking on the smoke. My wife, she jumped up. I didn't know what happened to her. I don't know what to do now. I see we are choking, and the heat, it was getting unbearable. And I hear my children hollering, Daddy, Daddy, Daddy, Daddy. And I couldn't reach them, because the heat was impossible, and the smoke. The heat was horrible. Here-- I thought to myself, at least this one, this one. I went, again, God-- I broke in the window. And I took this-- people downstairs already were hollering, throw him down, throw him down. We are going to catch him in the air. I said to him-- we were on the terrace-- I said to him, I says-- I still hear my children. I still hear them now, hollering for help. And I couldn't get them-- Daddy, Daddy, Daddy. I throw him down. They caught him in the air. He got some scratches. And I jumped down. My wife is nowhere available. She jumped in the fire in her nightgown. The nightgown was burning on her 65% of her body. Her face, her hair, her underarms, hands-- everything was burnt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [BACKGROUND NOISE] we were-- somehow, everywhere there's a miracle. Somehow, she found herself her way to the basement. And they were waiting. And the nightgown burned on her-- on her body. And the-- the ambulances with the firemen were there. And they grabbed her, and they took her to the hospital right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were-- I fell down from the second floor. I don't know, I was in a shock or whatever. They grabbed me, my son. He was then about eight years old. And they took us into the hospital. They tied us up on a bed, my son near me here. And we were crying out. They know nothing about my wife. I am asking about my wife. So they tell me they don't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Finally, they let me see my wife. But my wife, they were working on her. It was a Korean skin specialist. What he patched up the American soldiers when they were burned in the tanks, or they  were shot out in the tanks. And he was a specialist, and he was working on my wife. Bandage-- you know-- all over. Her face, she looked like a mummy, only her eyes, like a mummy. Only her eyes looked out. When I saw her, it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slowly by slowly, and I'm talking fast-- you know? Slowly by slowly, you know, I had to go to make arrangements to bury my two children. When they brought down my children, I was downstairs. They brought them down on a blanket. And I said, are they survived? They made with their head, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in, and I put my hand in that blanket on their face. It was like a burned steak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- what did you and your wife do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, she was horrible. But the energy, her healing energy should heal first her face is the way he worked. He opened up all her head and her face, and he was working with a kind of medication what he had. And that slowly by slowly to clear up. And then, he started every finger separately. And then he started the feet separately. You know, every three, four days they should reveal to the air because air heals. And she was getting better. I had to make arrangements for burial of the two children. I'm taking my son with me, because wherever he didn't want to remain alone. And we are getting in a taxi, which will go to a funeral parlour to make the arrangements of burying the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: He starts hitting me, and he hits me. We were crying, we were crying continuously. Here non-Jews, Gentiles, were calling into the priest in Atlantic City, what can we do to that couple? It was full. The newspapers were full. What can we do to that couple? I said, nothing. Just pray to God she should survive, because the doctor didn't give her more than a week to  live. Here we are going to make arrangements, and he hits me, and I says to him, Milton, why do you hit me? Aren't we punished enough? Didn't God punish me enough? You have to punish me too? You hit me after all of the hurts? Then he breaks out crying. He says, you taught me always that God loves little children. How did he love me? He says, he didn't leave me not even one who should play with, who I should play with. I saw his problems, and I had my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my wife, after eight weeks, she comes out of the hospital. The face, beautiful. They gave her a makeup. You know, I come in the hospital one day. I did not recognize my wife there. Gave her a makeup. You know, I was afraid that she's going to be a mummy, an Egyptian mummy. They say, she's coming out of the hospital. The doctor told me, he says, hire a woman to her, because she has a suicidal mania.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's coming home. She's offering me, said do me a favor, she says, let's go to Israel and adopt two  children, the same children like the-- I says, what do you think, Israel is going to give you children? Israel brought children from Europe, but they remained orphans, not because they should send them back to another strange country. And then those children, are they going to call you mother? They're never going to call you mother. Your children called you mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, let's drink a coffee-- arsenic. All the three of us will drink a coffee, and that's all to it. I says   to her, I says, listen darling, I have the right to my life. I can do whatever I want with it. You, too. But we haven't got the right to his life. He doesn't know. He's not big enough to know what he wants to do with his life. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's the way-- that is the way I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you and you wife do? You had another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hired a woman. Psychiatrist calls me on the side. Take us, both of us, and then separately. Psychiatrist says to me, says, listen Mr. Guttman. If you would have another child, she would be all right, because that child would smile in her face, and she wouldn't cry. The woman doesn't want to work no more for me because she said, your wife is crying. I'm crying, too. I know your troubles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I took her into the-- into the diner, that she should be with me by the cash register. I offered my wife, I says listen what the psychiatrist said, we should have another child. I says, if you haven't got no other tools, one nail is going to pull out the other nail. I says, let's have another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: She slapped my face. She says, what? God should take them away! I says, no, lightning doesn't strike twice in the same place. I says, let's have another child. No, she slapped me, she kicked me, she hit me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I, as a man, did whatever I had to do. And thanks God, the recipe came, a little girl. And as little girl, God bless her, she pulled us out of the doldrums, all the three of us. She smiled, and she was talking-- our world. And he took care of her, my son took care of her, and she-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Thank you. And thanks God, here we are. I have five, grandchildren. God bless them. And my children are very, very good to us, very good to us. So my daughter, so my son, they wouldn't know what to do with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before we meet your children, is there any message that you want to give with this tape to the people who watch it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like the world should learn, because those people, what they want to get away with that say that it never happened, then that means I was born from a rock, or I fell down from the moon like a crater. My parents, my sisters, my siblings-- all of them are gone in the flames. And oh, yes, it was a Holocaust. 6 million of our dear ones. I saw what I saw, and there's lots of things to talk. But the tape is not long enough. If the oceans would be ink, I'm afraid they would run dry, I could write what I saw. I saw children, grabbing them arriving, the Nazi grabbing them by the leg and throwing them against the cement wall, that their brains remain there. And then, they grab them by the leg and throw them on the truck to the burial ground. What I saw, somebody wants to get away with it, to say it never happened. Oh no. Here we are old people, slowly by slowly we are dying out. And no more witnesses. They're not going to remain witnesses. I came to bear witness of my problems of my past, of my catastrophe. That's the way my children don't have no uncles, no cousins. They have cousins among themselves because I have two children. But no uncles, no aunts, no grandparents. They want robbed out of the love from their grandparents and so on, and so on. I try to give my grandchildren what their parents didn't have. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Guttman, could you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On my left is my dear wife, what I married 50 years is going to be December the 13th. Thanks God, thanks to her. And she made me a good husband, and she made me a better wife than I am a husband. And to my-- after her, is my-- oh excuse me. On my right here is my son Milton, what I-- like I told you, like we saved each other. On my far left is my daughter Elaine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie. That is the prescription from the doctor, the recipe from the doctor. She pulled us out of the doldrums. And then I have, behind me, I have hear my two grandchildren, my two granddaughters, 16-year-old Audrey and Vicki, beautiful. And I love them like my life. And here, the way you see, we have this little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Big girl. Big girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Allison, six years old, my granddaughter. And I have my pair of  twins here, boys, also my daughter's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Charlie.

speaker: interviewee
text: We live-- that is Charlie and Jeremy. We live for them now, because who knows how long more. Thanks God for them. We live for them. And I thank you and your company-- for Steven Spielberg, for taking all this witness about our catastrophe where we went through-- through the 6 million people. And I thank you very much, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it the other day. But I give it-- I'd like my daughter to talk now, because she want it so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to talk Milton?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I wanted to say was that the-- the Holocaust, I think, affected the generation after in a very significant way. In my-- in me and my brother's situation, we never had any grandparents. We never had aunts, we never had uncles. We didn't have any cousins. We grew up almost family-less. That affected us in a lot of ways. And I think also, in our upbringing, especially the way that my parents had brought us up, was always in a way of fright against non-Jews, which is something that was-- it was very evident in our growing up that there was always in the back of their minds-- don't play, don't do. You know, it was a fright that they tried to instill in us, which was, in a way-- in my case, I think it reversed it. I think it didn't cause a fright. I think it opened my mind a little bit more so, and it gave me more of a sensitivity for it. But in all, the devastation that took place, it affected each and every one. I mean, I took it in college a different way also. And I became an activist. I decided to get involved in some activist groups in college. And that was my way of reaching out to find out more and learning more. And then, as I got married, I became more religious. And that was my other way. It was in trying to find, there was something that was lacking. It was like a need, a void that had to be filled. And it's been a terrible-- it-- I know that from friends and peers who are also children from Holocaust survivors, a lot of them have had problems in growing up. They haven't-- there's been like a lack. There's been a space, there's been a void as to, I don't know, even religion, or belief, or faith. And it's been a very difficult task. And these groups, with these generation after type groups, are very, very helpful. And I think that what you're doing here is marvelous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Is there anything you  want to say to you grandparents? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you love your grandparents? You love Baba and Zaide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allison

speaker: interviewee
text: Allison, say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you can talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell Alison something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell her.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: How about you? How about Baba and Zaide?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zesu.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zesu. That's a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know why you were named Zesu Allie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was Zaide's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know what? And Allison comes from Baba's sister. So you really get it all. And then, Vicki gets one, and Audry got one. It really went around. So I guess we're all-- the ones that are not here are still-- memorialized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something? Don't be shy.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about Milty. You want to say something? Milty, say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have nothing to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: He has nothing to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they have time, talk a little bit longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK, I don't need to talk anymore, but I appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
